title: Peter Cushing
content:
{{Top|fa|rwp}} {{Otheruses|Peter}} {{Person |image=[[File:Closeuptarkin.jpg]] |name=Peter Wilton Cushing |birth=[[May 26]], [[1910s#1913|1913]]
[[Wikipedia:Kenley|Kenley]], [[Wikipedia:Surrey|Surrey]], [[Wikipedia:England|England]]{{WebCite|url=https://www.nytimes.com/1994/08/12/obituaries/peter-cushing-actor-dies-at-81-known-for-playing-frankenstein.html|text=Peter Cushing, Actor, Dies at 81; Known for Playing Frankenstein|date=1994-08-12|work=[[Wikipedia:The New York Times|''The New York Times'']]|archivedate=20220220191034}} |death=[[August 11]], [[1994]]
[[Wikipedia:Canterbury|Canterbury]], [[Wikipedia:Kent|Kent]], England |listedpronouns= |nationality=[[Wikipedia:British people|British]] |occupation=[[Wikipedia:Actor|Actor]] |companies= |website= }} {{Quote|My criterion for accepting a role isn't based on what ''I'' would like to do. I try to consider what the ''audience'' would like to see me do. I thought kids would adore ''Star Wars'', since they love all things about outer space.|Peter Cushing|{{Starlog|96|Starlog (magazine)|Peter Cushing: The Baddie With a Heart of Gold}}}} '''Peter Wilton Cushing''' [[Wikipedia:Order of the British Empire|OBE]] ([[May 26]], [[1910s#1913|1913]] – [[August 11]], [[1994]]) was a [[Wikipedia:British people|British]] [[Wikipedia:Actor|actor]] who gained wide recognition for his roles in the [[Wikipedia:Hammer Film Productions|Hammer Studios]] horror films of the [[1950s]] to the 1970s alongside [[Christopher Lee]]. When developing the [[1977]] film [[Star Wars: Episode IV A New Hope|''Star Wars'']], [[George Lucas]] cast Cushing in the role of [[Grand Moff]] [[Wilhuff Tarkin|Tarkin]] to provide a human counterpart for the primary villain, [[Anakin Skywalker|Darth Vader]], although Cushing claimed he was approached for the role of the noble [[Jedi Master]] [[Obi-Wan Kenobi]]. Cushing accepted the role on the basis that he felt his fans would enjoy seeing him in a science fiction film. His part in the film was recorded over a two-week period in [[May]] [[1976]]. The boots created for Cushing's character were ill-suited for the size of his feet, leading the actor to ask Lucas to let him wear slippers and to shoot his scenes from the waist up. Cushing was a courteous but good-humored figure and became well-liked among the film's other cast members, with [[Carrie Fisher]] commenting that she found it difficult to act as though she hated his character. He admitted that he struggled with the level of technical jargon and special effects used in the film but worked hard to ensure that his character's dialogue sounded confident. Given Cushing's well-known status, he was paid a higher salary than the film's lead actors—Fisher, [[Mark Hamill]], and [[Harrison Ford]]—and promotional materials presented Tarkin as the main villain. Cushing enjoyed the film greatly and was proud of its success, describing his only disappointment as that his character's death meant that he could not reprise the role in the sequels. Cushing's involvement in ''Star Wars'' later influenced Lee's participation in the [[prequel trilogy]] films on account of their friendship. In the [[2016]] film ''[[Rogue One: A Star Wars Story]]'', Cushing's likeness was recreated via motion capture over the performance of [[Guy Henry]] as Tarkin, with permission granted by Cushing's estate. His portrayal of Tarkin influenced the performances of several other actors in ''[[Star Wars]]'' projects, including [[Stephen Stanton]], [[Richard E. Grant]], and [[Giancarlo Esposito]]. ==Biography== ===Early life and career=== {{Quote|I suppose I couldn't help becoming well known, because my Hammer films are shown on television ''somewhere'' in the world nearly every day.|Peter Cushing|}} [[File:Peter_Cushing_as_Frankenstein.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Peter Cushing achieved notoriety for his performances in Hammer horror films, including ''The Curse of Frankenstein''.]] Peter Wilton Cushing was born in [[Wikipedia:Kenley|Kenley]], a district in the [[Wikipedia:England|English]] county of [[Wikipedia:Surrey|Surrey]], on [[May 26]], [[1910s#1913|1913]]. He was widely known for roles in numerous [[Wikipedia:Horror film|horror films]] made by the [[Wikipedia:United Kingdom|British]] [[Wikipedia:Hammer Film Productions|Hammer Studios]] throughout the [[1950s]] to the 1970s. His most prominent role was that of the villainous [[Wikipedia:Victor Frankenstein|Baron Victor Frankenstein]] in [[Wikipedia:The Curse of Frankenstein|''The Curse of Frankenstein'']] and its sequels. Cushing appeared alongside [[Christopher Lee]] in almost twenty-five films, and the two became very close friends.{{InsiderCite|51|Christopher Lee: Rings of Fire}} Although not a particular fan of [[Wikipedia:Science fiction|science fiction]], Cushing starred in several films of the genre throughout his career, including two films in the [[1960s]] based on the popular [[Wikipedia:British people|British]] television series [[Wikipedia:Doctor Who|''Doctor Who'']]. He also portrayed the role of [[Wikipedia:Sherlock Holmes|Sherlock Holmes]] in a 1965 [[Wikipedia:Sherlock Holmes (1965 TV series)|television series]]. Cushing's wife, Helen Beck, died in [[1971]],{{InsiderCite|37|Peter Cushing: Charming to the Last}} causing Cushing to work in at least twenty films in the following five years as a way of dealing with his grief.{{BookCite|author=Earnshaw, Tony|container=Star Wars, Grand Moff Tarkin, and Peter Cushing|title=Infinity|number=48|date=2022-05|issn=2514-3654}} He would cycle to his wife's grave every day.''[[The Making of Star Wars: The Definitive Story Behind the Original Film]]'' ===''Star Wars''=== ====Casting==== {{Quote|After we started designing the costumes, and I saw what Darth Vader looked like, I felt I really needed a human villain, too, because you can't see Darth Vader's face. I got a little nervous about it, so I wanted somebody really strong, a really good villain—and actually Peter Cushing was my first choice on that.|George Lucas|}} [[File:Tarkin_DS.jpg|thumb|left|300px|Peter Cushing as Grand Moff Wilhuff Tarkin in ''Star Wars'']] In early [[1976]], film director and writer [[George Lucas]] approached Cushing with the hopes of casting the actor in his upcoming space fantasy film, [[Star Wars: Episode IV A New Hope|''Star Wars'']]. Since the film's primary antagonist, [[Anakin Skywalker|Darth Vader]], played by [[David Prowse]], wore a mask throughout the entire film and his face was never visible, Lucas felt a strong [[human]] villain character was necessary. This led Lucas to expand the character of [[Grand Moff]] [[Wilhuff Tarkin|Tarkin]], a high-ranking [[Galactic Empire|Imperial]] [[governor]] and [[Commanding officer|commander]] of the [[planet]]-destroying [[Battle station/Legends|battlestation]] named the [[DS-1 Orbital Battle Station|Death Star]]. Lucas felt a talented actor was needed to play the role and was inspired to select Cushing by a photograph of him and Lee at the Hammer Film studios headquarters, which were in the same building as Lucas's office.{{BookCite|author=Rigby, Jonathan|container=Foreword|title=Christopher Lee: The Authorized Screen History|publisher=Reynolds & Hearn|date=2001|isbn=1903111110}} It was later speculated that Lucas cast Cushing due to his resemblance to actor [[Wikipedia:Charles Middleton (actor)|Charles Middleton]], the actor who portrayed the villainous [[Wikipedia:Ming the Merciless|Emperor Ming]] in the [[Wikipedia:Flash Gordon (serial)|''Flash Gordon'']] serials of the [[1930s]] that had inspired Lucas in creating ''Star Wars''.''[[The Star Wars Album]]'' Lucas stated Peter Cushing was his first choice for the part of Tarkin. However, Cushing later claimed that Lucas originally approached him to play the [[Jedi Master]] [[Obi-Wan Kenobi]] and only decided to cast him as Tarkin instead after the two met each other. Cushing said he would have preferred to play Kenobi rather than Tarkin, but he could not have done so because he was working on another film when ''Star Wars'' was shooting, and Tarkin's scenes took less time to film than those of the larger Kenobi role, which [[Alec Guinness]] filled. Cushing compared the film to ''Doctor Who'' and accepted the part as his criteria for selecting roles was based on what he believed his audience, particularly children, would enjoy seeing. Cushing was in his sixties at the time of filming and, as such, was flattered to learn that he was still desired as an actor. Along with Guinness, Cushing was among the most famous actors at the time to appear in ''Star Wars'', as the rest of the cast was still relatively unknown. As a result, Cushing was paid a larger daily salary than most of his fellow cast, earning £2,000 British pound sterling—approximately $3,656 in American dollars at the timeAccording to {{WebCite|url=https://www.poundsterlinglive.com/bank-of-england-spot/historical-spot-exchange-rates/gbp/GBP-to-USD-1976|text=British Pound / US Dollar Historical Reference Rates from Bank of England for 1976|work=[https://www.poundsterlinglive.com Pound Sterling Live]|archivedate=20240111041151}}, the conversion rate from [[Wikipedia:Pound sterling|British pound sterling]] to the [[Wikipedia:United States dollar|United States dollar]] as of [[May 3]], [[1976]] was 1.8282. When that number is multiplied by 2,000, the product is 3,656.4.—per day compared to weekly salaries of $1,000 for [[Mark Hamill]] ([[Luke Skywalker]]), $850 for [[Carrie Fisher]] ([[Leia Skywalker Organa Solo|Leia Organa]]) and $750 for [[Harrison Ford]] ([[Han Solo]]). ====Costume, performance, and rehearsals==== {{Quote|So, there I was, stomping around, shouting orders to cut people's heads off right and left, and I was really wearing ''carpet slippers''. The next time you watch ''Star Wars'', notice how seldom you see my feet. And when you do, I hope you will realize the ''torture'' I was going through. ''That'' is why Moff Tarkin was so hostile all the time—his feet were ''killing'' him.|Peter Cushing|}} During rehearsals, Lucas originally planned for Tarkin and Vader to use a giant screen filled with computerized architectural representations of hallways to monitor the whereabouts of the film's protagonists, Luke Skywalker, Han Solo, and Leia Organa. Although the idea was abandoned before filming began, Cushing and Prowse rehearsed those scenes in a set built by computer animation artist [[Larry Cuba]]. Like Guinness, Cushing had difficulty with some of the technical jargon in his dialogue, and claimed he did not understand all of the words he was speaking. Nevertheless, he worked hard to master the lines so they would sound natural and that his character would appear intelligent and confident. In interpreting the villainous role, Cushing relied on Lucas's input and his own instincts as an actor. Cushing was uncomfortable in his character's [[Imperial officer's uniform|uniform]], telling Lucas that he felt he resembled a chaffeur. He was provided with a pair of boots far too small to accommodate the actor's size twelve feet. This caused a great deal of pain for him during shooting sessions, but the costume designers did not have enough time to get him another pair. As a result, he asked Lucas to film more close-up shots of him from the waist up, to which the director agreed. Cushing wore the only available footwear, a pair of women's slippers, during the scenes where his feet were not visible. When Cushing smoked between shots, he wore a white glove so the make-up artists would not have to deal with nicotine stains on his fingers.{{InsiderCite|94|Tribute: Peter Cushing}} ====Filming==== {{Quote|But I liked Peter Cushing so much that, in my mind, I had to substitute somebody else in order to get the hatred for him. I had to say, 'I recognized your foul stench…' But the man smelled like linen and lavender.|Carrie Fisher|}} [[File:Peter_Cushing_George_Lucas_Carrie_Fisher_filming_Star_Wars.jpg|thumb|right|300px|Director George Lucas (middle) instructs Peter Cushing and actress Carrie Fisher on the set of ''Star Wars''.]] Cushing joined the rest of the cast in [[May]] 1976, with his scenes recorded in [[Elstree Studios]] in [[Wikipedia:Borehamwood|Borehamwood]], England, over two weeks. On [[April 29]], [[May 3]], and [[May 4]],''[[The Star Wars Archives: Episodes IV–VI, 1977–1983]]'' Cushing filmed the scene of the [[Destruction of Alderaan|destruction of Alderaan]] with Fisher set in the Death Star control room, which served as her first major dialogue in the film. Cushing consciously attempted to define their characters as opposite representations of good and evil, and the actor purposely stood in the shadows so the light would shine on Fisher's face. He mentored Fisher, who was in her first major film role, providing advice and explanations on what to avoid in filmmaking. According to [[Richard LeParmentier]], who played [[General]] [[Conan Antonio Motti|Motti]], Fisher would often attempt to get a rise out of Cushing through humor, contributing to the positive shooting environment.{{InsiderCite|40|In the Star Wars Universe|In the ''Star Wars'' Universe: Richard LeParmentier}} To portray the hatred Organa's character had for Tarkin, Fisher tried to imagine Cushing as a different person, as she was so fond of him that she found that emotion difficult to perform. Although one of Fisher's lines refers to Tarkin's "foul stench," she said the actual actor smelled like "linen and lavender," something Cushing attributed to his tendency to wash and brush his teeth thoroughly before filming because of his self-consciousness about bad breath. Upon learning of that line, Cushing asked Lucas, "Do you want me to look as if I have body odor?" During filming of the scene of a boardroom meeting between [[Imperial officer]]s on May 3,{{InsiderCite|96|Set Piece|Set Piece: The Death Star Conference Room}} the other actors, including [[Don Henderson]] as [[Cassio Tagge]], found it difficult to focus on Cushing's villainous performance due to the slippers he was wearing. The actors had to stifle their laughter while looking Cushing in the eyes, which caused Cushing some concern as he was unsure what he was doing wrong. When the scene was completed, the other actors told Cushing about the difficulty they had experienced, causing him to roar with laughter. Cushing found it challenging to work with the significant level of special effects in the film, feeling it somewhat undermined the level of effort actors put into learning their lines. [[File:Mark_Hamill_Peter_Cushing.png|thumb|left|300px|Mark Hamill and Peter Cushing interacting on the set of ''Star Wars'']] Cushing enjoyed collaborating with Prowse again, as they had worked together in the [[1974]] film [[Wikipedia:Frankenstein and the Monster from Hell|''Frankenstein and the Monster from Hell'']], where Prowse had played [[Wikipedia:Frankenstein's monster|Frankenstein's monster]]. Cushing commented that Prowse's [[Darth Vader's armor|costume]] was much more intimidating than his monster costume, which he said was merely a "bald cap and a diaper." In contrast to his character's cruel nature, Cushing was highly esteemed by his coworkers, remembered as a "sweetheart" by Fisher and as an old-fashioned, charming but humorous gentleman by Henderson. He liked the environment on set in turn, admiring the professionalism of his younger co-stars and appreciating Lucas's respectful demeanor, which he had not anticipated from a young filmmaker. Mark Hamill was disappointed when he realized he had no scenes with Cushing, being a fan of his,{{InsiderCite|122|The Return of the Jedi: Mark Hamill at Celebration V}} so he visited the set on a day off to meet him and ask for an autograph. Hamill asked questions about Cushing's past acting career, specifically what it was like working with the comedy duo [[Wikipedia:Laurel and Hardy|Laurel and Hardy]] in the [[1940s#1940|1940]] film [[Wikipedia:A Chump at Oxford|''A Chump at Oxford'']]. Cushing was surprised that he had even heard of the film. Lucas found Cushing highly pleasant to work with, leading the director to continue using British actors who could play larger-than-life villains in his films. ====Post-production and release==== {{Quote|I was absolutely knocked for six, I was riveted. ''Star Wars'' was a picture you had to see again, to take in so much you missed the first time. Even the beginning was incredible, with that tablet of words receding like a pyramid into time. My only disappointment was that poor old Tarkin was blown up at the end, which meant I couldn't appear in the sequels.|Peter Cushing's thoughts on ''Star Wars''|}} [[File:TarkinBattleOfYavin.png|thumb|right|250px|Scenes of Tarkin watching the Battle of Yavin (pictured) were not originally scripted, but were added during the editing phase.]] According to [[Rick McCallum]], producer of the [[The Star Wars Trilogy Special Edition|special editions]] of the [[Original trilogy|original ''Star Wars'' trilogy]], every scene that Cushing filmed was included in the final film.[[Star Wars: The Galaxy's Greatest Villains|''Star Wars'': The Galaxy's Greatest Villains]] Additionally, the close-up shots of Cushing aboard the Death Star, shown right before the battlestation is destroyed, were actually extra footage taken from previously shot scenes with Cushing that had been shortened. During production, Lucas decided to add those shots, along with [[Wikipedia:Second unit|second unit]] footage of the Death Star [[Imperial gunner/Legends|gunners]] preparing to fire, to add more suspense to the film's space battle scenes. Cushing's role as Tarkin was first publicly revealed in a promotional blurb published in the [[Wikipedia:Variety (magazine)|''Daily Variety'']] magazine on [[June 15]], 1976. Due to Cushing's fame in the Hammer horror films, the blurb and other promotional materials presented him as the main villain, rather than Vader.''[[Star Wars: The Complete Vader]]'' ''Star Wars'' premiered on [[May 25]], [[1977]], and became a global phenomenon and the highest grossing film at that time.''[[Star Wars Year By Year: A Visual History, Updated and Expanded Edition]]'' Cushing was extremely pleased with the final film and proud of its success, stating that his only disappointment was that Tarkin was killed and could not appear in the [[Star Wars: Episode V The Empire Strikes Back|subsequent]] [[Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi|sequels]]. He believed his own performance was only passable at best, given that he found it difficult to watch and evaluate his own work. Christopher Lee wrote Cushing a letter asking him about the film, including a question about what a Grand Moff was, to which Cushing admitted that he still had no idea. For his performance, Cushing was nominated for the [[Wikipedia:Saturn Award for Best Supporting Actor|Saturn Award for Best Supporting Actor]] at the [[Wikipedia:5th Saturn Awards|5th Saturn Awards]], which were conducted by the [[Wikipedia:Academy of Science Fiction, Fantasy and Horror Films|Academy of Science Fiction, Fantasy and Horror Films]]. Cushing did not win the award, which went to his co-star Guinness instead.{{IMDb|url=event/ev0000004/1978/1|text=Academy of Science Fiction, Fantasy & Horror Films, USA — 1978 Awards|archivedate=20221216094428}} ===Later years and death=== {{Quote|Unfortunately, fans of the ''Star Wars'' trilogy won't get a chance to see him in another collaboration with George Lucas. But Cushing's amazing lifetime of work will be on the silver screen for his fans and future fans to appreciate.|A tribute to Cushing in ''Star Wars Insider''|{{InsiderCite|24|A Look at Peter Cushing}}}} Around [[July]] [[1985]], Cushing participated in a lengthy interview with the [[Starlog (magazine)|''Starlog'']] magazine, in which he reflected on his career, including ''Star Wars''. In it, Cushing rebuffed complaints made by Prowse that the supporting actors in ''Star Wars'' were not equitably compensated, given that Hamill, Fisher, Ford, and Guinness received substantial bonuses after the film's enormous success. Cushing felt that his original payment was appropriate and was still surprised to receive a "token bonus." In [[January]] [[1989]], Cushing was made an [[Wikipedia:Order of the British Empire|Officer of the Order of the British Empire]] by [[Wikipedia:Monarchy of the United Kingdom|Queen]] [[Wikipedia:Elizabeth II|Elizabeth II]] for his services to the film industry.[[Star Wars Year by Year: A Visual Chronicle (updated edition)|''Star Wars Year by Year: A Visual Chronicle'']] Cushing died in [[Wikipedia:Canterbury|Canterbury]], [[Wikipedia:Kent|Kent]], England, on [[August 11]], [[1994]], aged eighty-one. The [[Star Wars Insider 24|twenty-fourth]] issue of the magazine ''[[Star Wars Insider]]'', released in December 1994, included a tribute to him by writer [[Jamie Painter]] that referenced his wider career. ==Legacy== ===Prequel trilogy=== {{Quote|He meant a great deal to me in my life, not just as an actor but as a person. We were very close friends, and I still miss him very much. That will be one very important reason why I will be so happy to do this picture. Because I will be following him.|Christopher Lee, on the impact Peter Cushing had on him and his participation in ''Star Wars''|}} [[File:YoungTarkin-SWCTP.png|thumb|left|250px|Wayne Pygram (pictured) wore makeup to resemble Peter Cushing for ''Revenge of the Sith''.]] While developing the [[prequel trilogy]] film [[Star Wars: Episode II Attack of the Clones|''Star Wars'': Episode II ''Attack of the Clones'']] and creating the character of [[Count of Serenno|Count]] [[Dooku]], Lucas realized he wanted another gentlemanly and aristocratic actor in the vein of Cushing.{{InsiderCite|52|George Lucas: The Story Comes First}} He thus offered the part to Christopher Lee, who was delighted to accept on account of his friendship with Cushing. His casting was announced on [[StarWars.com]] on [[July 11]], [[2000]],{{SWArchive|url=episode-ii/bts/production/news20000711.html|text=Casting Update: Christopher Lee}} and ''Attack of the Clones'' was released in [[2002]]. Lee also appeared in the third film of the trilogy, [[Star Wars: Episode III Revenge of the Sith|''Star Wars'': Episode III ''Revenge of the Sith'']], which included a brief appearance by Tarkin at its ending.{{Film|III}} Lucas and producer Rick McCallum initially wanted to recreate Cushing's likeness using [[Wikipedia:Computer-generated imagery|computer-generated imagery]] or by transposing his younger appearance from a ''Frankenstein'' film.{{InsiderCite|87|Skywalking|Skywalking: Alien Encounters: The Makeup of ''Revenge of the Sith''}} Using unused footage from ''Star Wars'' and digitally animating Cushing's lips to accommodate new dialogue was also considered.''[[The Making of Star Wars Revenge of the Sith]]'' Special effects artist [[Rob Coleman]] was worried at the prospect of digitally recreating Cushing, discussing it at length with Lee on account of his friendship with Cushing.{{Film|III}} audio commentary with [[George Lucas]], [[Rick McCallum]], [[Rob Coleman]], [[John Knoll]], and [[Roger Guyett]] Instead, makeup artist [[Dave Elsey]] proposed that Tarkin be played by an actor wearing makeup, suggesting [[Wayne Pygram]] for the part due to his initial resemblance and ability to recreate Cushing's mannerisms. Pygram's initial makeup tests involved prosthetics for his chin, cheeks, and nose. After their demonstration, Lucas and McCallum agreed with their solution and contacted Cushing's estate to make the necessary arrangements. At one point in production, Elsey applied makeup for actor [[Ian McDiarmid]] while they watched Cushing's ''Sherlock Holmes'' television series. Lee happened to drop by while they watched the series, which excited Elsey as a fan of Cushing's work.{{Starlog|332|Starlog (magazine)|Faces of Revenge}} ===Animation=== {{Quote|Considering it was ''The Clone Wars'' and it was a younger Tarkin, I went back and referenced Peter Cushing's films from the 1950s. Especially the stuff that he did when he was doing films for Hammer. And I actually settled on a BBC TV series he had done in the '60s on Sherlock Holmes, because I thought the attitude really matched Tarkin in ''The Clone Wars''.|Stephen Stanton, on Cushing's influence on the younger version of Tarkin|{{SW|url=news/from-tarkin-to-raddus-star-wars-voice-actor-stephen-stanton-looks-back|text=From Tarkin to Raddus: ''Star Wars'' Voice Actor Stephen Stanton Looks Back}}}} [[File:Stephen_Stanton.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Stephen Stanton (pictured) took inspiration from Cushing's pre-''Star Wars'' roles to voice a younger version of Tarkin in various animated projects.]] Starting in [[2011]], Tarkin also appeared in the animated television series ''[[Star Wars: The Clone Wars]]''. The character was designed by sculptor [[Darren Marshall]], who based him on Cushing's image. Marshall said he grew up with the Hammer films and admired the talents and expressive faces of both Cushing and Lee.{{InsiderCite|124|Making Maquettes}} After gaining the chance to audition, voice actor [[Stephen Stanton]] realized that the creators were looking for a soundalike of a younger Cushing. As such, he rewatched several of Cushing's projects, including the 1966 film [[Wikipedia:Island of Terror|''Island of Terror'']], and the ''Sherlock Holmes'' television series. He also viewed Cushing's ''Doctor Who'' films on the recommendation of ''The Clone Wars'' showrunner [[Dave Filoni]], not initially realizing that Cushing was already playing an older character. Stanton intended to match Cushing's cadence in rolling his Rs and hoped to merge Cushing's ruthless portrayal of Tarkin with a younger man's vibrancy.{{InsiderCite|125|Talking Tarkin!}} For additional research, Stanton read Cushing's autobiographies and listened to Cushing's audiobook versions of them. Given that Cushing was already middle-aged when his film career achieved success, Stanton had to imagine what his mid-30s self would have sounded like. Stanton felt nervous taking the role, given the iconic status of both Cushing and Tarkin.{{SWArchive|url=theclonewars/stephen_stanton_as_tarkin/index.html|text=Look Who's Tarkin: Stephen Stanton|author=Vilmur, Pete|date=2011-03-03}} Stanton reprised the role in the animated series ''[[Star Wars Rebels]]'' and was able to match his voice closer to Cushing's performance in the original ''Star Wars'' film, given the show's closer timeline to the events of that film. ===''Tarkin'' (novel)=== {{Quote|I grew up with the Hammer Horror Films, and they were really imprinted on me. I pulled out a few of those films before I did Tarkin just to give me a sense of Peter Cushing and a little about the way he delivered his lines. I wanted to try and stay true to that, the character that we will end up meeting in ''A New Hope''.|James Luceno, on writing the novel ''Tarkin''|{{WebCite|url=http://archive.alternativenation.net/interview-author-james-luceno-talks-star-wars-tarkin-teases-palpatine-novel|text=Interview: Author James Luceno Talks 'Star Wars: Tarkin', Teases Palpatine Novel|author=McCausland, Doug|date=2014-12-10|archivedate=20180327114419}}}} Following [[The Walt Disney Company]]'s acquisition of the ''Star Wars'' franchise,{{SW|url=news/new-star-wars-movies-announced-as-disney-enters-agreement-to-acquire-lucasfilm-ltd|text=New ''Star Wars'' Movies Announced as Disney Enters Agreement to Acquire Lucasfilm Ltd.}} a novel titled [[Tarkin (novel)|''Tarkin'']] by [[James Luceno]] was announced, to be published by [[Del Rey]] on [[November 4]], [[2014]].{{SW|url=news/disney-publishing-worldwide-and-random-house-announce-relaunch-of-star-wars-adult-fiction-line|text=Disney Publishing Worldwide and Random House Announce Relaunch of Star Wars Adult Fiction Line}} Luceno grew up watching Cushing's Hammer horror films and watched several films starring the actor, including ''Frankenstein'', ''Doctor Who'', and ''Sherlock Holmes'',{{WebCite|url=http://www.denofgeek.com/us/books-comics/star-wars/241265/james-luceno-interview-star-wars-the-sith-tarkin-palpatines-first-name|text=James Luceno Interview: Star Wars, the Sith, Tarkin & Palpatine's First Name|author=Crouse, Megan|date=2014-11-13|archivedate=20161012134705}} as he wanted to capture a sense of Cushing's line delivery. One line of dialogue from the novel included Tarkin expressing desire for a uniform and pair of boots that fit.[[Tarkin (novel)|''Tarkin'']] Luceno enjoyed writing a scene where Tarkin and Dooku met each other, finding it enjoyable to visualize Cushing and Lee acting alongside each other within ''Star Wars'' as they had done in the Hammer horror films.{{InsiderCite|153|Tarkin Revealed}} ===''Rogue One'' recreation=== ====Casting==== {{Quote|Obviously the people making Rogue One made it clear that this was an extraordinary experiment, something that had never been done before in the history of film, and I was daunted by the whole thing. I didn't want to let down Lucasfilm and Disney nor the Star Wars fans, but particularly I didn't want to let down Peter Cushing, whom I'd always greatly admired. To be chosen to represent him was an honour.|Guy Henry, on being chosen to portray Tarkin while being edited to resemble Peter Cushing through visual effects|{{WebCite|url=https://starwarsinterviews.com/star-wars-anthology/rogue-one/guy-henry-tarkin|text=Guy Henry (Tarkin) – ''Star Wars'' Interviews|format=Interview|work=[https://starwarsinterviews.com ''Star Wars'' Interviews]|archivedate=20200705022034|nolive=1}}}} During production of the [[2016]] film ''[[Rogue One: A Star Wars Story]]'', which follows the [[Battle of Scarif|theft]] of the [[Death Star plans]] that occurred shortly before the events of the original ''Star Wars'' film, the film's writers realized that Tarkin was an essential character to their film's story. Director [[Gareth Edwards]] approached [[Industrial Light & Magic]] (ILM) lead [[John Knoll]] to ask if the idea of digitally recreating Cushing's likeness was unreasonable, to which Knoll responded that the company's purpose was to push the boundaries of visual effects forward.{{SW|url=news/gareth-edwards-rogue-one-interview|text=Gareth Edwards Looks Back on ''Rogue One: A Star Wars Story''}} The filmmakers gained permission from Cushing's estate to use his likeness. [[File:Tarkin-Rogue-One.jpg|thumb|left|300px|The finished recreation of Peter Cushing as Tarkin]] Casting director [[Jina Jay]] sent Edwards a clip of actor [[Guy Henry]] for the role, noting a resemblance in his mannerisms to Cushing. Henry had played the role of Sherlock Holmes early in his career and as such had studied Cushing's performance as the same character closely,{{WebCite|url=https://www.empireonline.com/movies/features/rogue-one-13-revelations-from-director-gareth-edwards|text=Rogue One: 13 Revelations From Director Gareth Edwards|author=Green, Willow|date=2017-01-03|work=[[Wikipedia:Empire (magazine)|''Empire'']]|archivedate=20200801121733}} and he credited Cushing with inspiring his acting career. Edwards approached Henry for the role, knowing that it was a strange request to ask an actor to have his work hidden via another actor's face. Henry agreed on account of Cushing's influence on him, stating that he would have refused if he had been asked to perform as any other actor. To accurately imitate Cushing, Henry watched scenes of Cushing repeatedly and attempted to mimic his voice effectively, believing the rolled Rs were key. Before each take, Henry would repeat Tarkin's line "You would prefer another target, a military target?" from the original ''Star Wars'' to slip in to the character. Henry found the process daunting and did not want to let Cushing down. In directing him, Edwards suggested that Henry could act more like himself rather than Cushing. Henry also suggested that impressionist [[Wikipedia:Rory Bremner|Rory Bremner]] may have been a more suitable choice, but Edwards and [[Lucasfilm]] insisted on retaining Henry. ====Visual effects and release==== {{Quote|This work was done with great affection and care… I'd like to think that the role we gave Tarkin in this film is one that Peter Cushing would have been excited and happy to play.|John Knoll|{{YouTube|video=xMB2sLwz0Do|text=How Rogue One Created Princess Leia, Grand Moff Tarkin|channel=@ABCNews|name=ABC News|date=2017-01-05}}}} [[File:Guy_Henry_Tarkin-Rogue_One.png|thumb|right|300px|Stages in recreating Cushing's likeness on top of Guy Henry's performance as Tarkin in ''Rogue One'']] To prepare for the recreation, the effects artists at ILM watched Tarkin's scenes from the original film repeatedly and referred back to them constantly throughout production. Knoll was also able to track down a life cast taken of Cushing for his role in the [[1984]] film [[Wikipedia:Top Secret!|''Top Secret!'']], which they digitally scanned to provide reference for Cushing's facial features around the time of 1977's ''Star Wars''. Henry wore motion capture equipment during filming, allowing ILM to make frame-by-frame adjustments to more closely match the simulations of Cushing as required. Adjustments also had to be made to compensate for differences in how ''Rogue One'' and ''Star Wars'' had been lit. The effects were adjusted over an eighteen-month period, although Edwards and the crew were unsure whether the visual effects would have worked as intended. Other options were considered in case the effects were not deemed adequate, such as representing Tarkin via [[hologram]] or giving his dialogue to other characters.{{WebCite|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2016/12/27/movies/how-rogue-one-brought-back-grand-moff-tarkin.html?_r=0|text=How 'Rogue One' Brought Back Familiar Faces|author=Itzkoff, Dave|date=2016-12-27|work=[[Wikipedia:The New York Times|''The New York Times'']]|archivedate=20230212071953}} The crew only became convinced that they had succeeded in the week prior to the film's release in 2016. Tarkin's appearance in the film was not explicitly revealed in promotional materials, although alleged leaks about Cushing's recreation were published in the media.{{WebCite|url=https://www.dailymail.co.uk/tvshowbiz/article-3207415/Dracula-legend-grave-Star-Wars-prequel-Peter-Cushing-digitally-recreated-new-spin-Rogue-One.html|text=Dracula legend back from the grave - for ''Star Wars'' prequel: Peter Cushing is set to be digitally recreated in new spin-off ''Rogue One''|author=Graham, Caroline|date=2015-08-23|work=[[Wikipedia:Daily Mail|''Daily Mail'']]|archivedate=20220218175212}} Several TV spots also provided fleeting glimpses of the character.{{WebCite|url=https://variety.com/2016/film/news/rogue-one-peter-cushing-digital-resurrection-cgi-1201943759|text=''Rogue One'': What Peter Cushing's Digital Resurrection Means for the Industry|author=Tapley, Kristopher, and Debruge, Peter|date=2016-12-16|work=[[Wikipedia:Variety (magazine)|''Variety'']]|archivedate=20170601062353}} Upon the film's release, Edwards was surprised that audiences had not expected the character to appear. The film's credits included a special acknowledgment to Cushing for the use of his likeness.''[[Rogue One: A Star Wars Story]]'' Cushing's former secretary and overseer of his estate, Joyce Brighton, was amazed at the digital effects, although she could not discuss the nature of the confidential agreement between the estate and Lucasfilm. The deceased actor's appearance in the film triggered discourse and debate in media publications about ethical concerns involved in using an actor's likeness without their physical presence. Knoll rebutted that the work in recreating Cushing was done with a high level of affection and care, which he believed Cushing would have appreciated; Knoll believed that the crew had not done anything of which Cushing would not have approved.{{WebCite|url=https://www.yahoo.com/entertainment/rogue-one-creator-defends-cgi-tarkin-says-carrie-fisher-loved-her-digital-self-203453637.html|text='Rogue One' Creator Defends CGI Tarkin; Says Carrie Fisher 'Loved' Her Digital Self (But She Won't Come Back)|author=Polowy, Kevin|date=2017-01-14|work=[[Wikipedia:Yahoo!|Yahoo!]]|archivedate=20230201130657}} Henry himself was relieved with the finished appearance of the character and said he would be happy to reprise the role if called for.{{YouTube|video=6l7-Pwtow_8|text=Guy Henry On Recreating Peter Cushing For Rouge One|channel=@RedCarpetNewsTV|name=Red Carpet News TV|date=2017-11-06}} ===Comics=== {{Quote|We were talking about our love of Christopher Lee and Peter Cushing, and how it would be great to see Tarkin and Dooku together, and fantastic if it was like a Frankenstein story. We started throwing around this idea of Tarkin creating a clone of Dooku.|Cavan Scott, on a conversation he and Michael Siglain had that led to a story in ''Star Wars Adventures: Return to Vader's Castle'' 2|{{InsiderCite|204|Cavan Scott's Wild Adventure}}}} [[File:SWA-ReturntoVadersCastle-2.jpg|thumb|left|250px|Author Cavan Scott took influence from Cushing's Hammer horror films in writing the comic ''Star Wars Adventures: Return to Vader's Castle'' 2.]] In [[2017]], Lucasfilm Publishing creative director [[Michael Siglain]] and author [[Cavan Scott]] discussed links between ''Star Wars'' and their favorite horror films, including the Hammer productions starring Cushing and Lee, and riffed on how those stories could be adapted creatively into the ''Star Wars'' universe, such as having Tarkin analogous to Dr. Frankenstein,{{SW|url=news/tales-from-the-death-star-interview|text=Cavan Scott on the Ghoulactic Horrors of ''Tales from the Death Star''}} or if Tarkin and Dooku could be connected in a narrative. The resulting idea was for Tarkin to create a [[Cloning|clone]] of Dooku. The concept evolved into the miniseries ''[[Star Wars Adventures: Return to Vader's Castle]]'', the structure of which—involving multiple stories with varying tones and monsters linked by a central character—was influenced by the Cushing/Lee film [[Wikipedia:Dr. Terror's House of Horrors|''Dr. Terror's House of Horrors'']].{{SW|url=news/sdcc-2018-tales-from-vaders-castle|text=SDCC 2018: IDW's ''Tales from Vader's Castle'' to Bring the Galactic Frights This October - Exclusive}} Scott's Tarkin-centered story was the [[Star Wars Adventures: Return to Vader's Castle 2|second issue]] of the series, published by [[IDW Publishing]] on [[October 9]], [[2019]].{{Comixology|url=Star-Wars-Adventures-Return-to-Vader-s-Castle-2-of-5/digital-comic/796196|text=Star Wars Adventures: Return to Vader's Castle #2|nolive=1}} The plot, subtitled "The Curse of Tarkin," involves the Grand Moff battling against an experimental [[Tarkin's creature|creature]] of his own making that desires revenge on him,[[Star Wars Adventures: Return to Vader's Castle 2|''Star Wars Adventures: Return to Vader's Castle'' 2]] which is similar to the plot of the novel [[Wikipedia:Frankenstein|''Frankenstein'']] by [[Wikipedia:Mary Shelley|Mary Shelley]].{{BookCite|author=Shelley, Mary|title=Frankenstein, or, The Modern Prometheus|version=Revised Edition|publisher=[[Wikipedia:Random House|Random House]]|date=1985|isbn=9780553212471|link=https://archive.org/details/frankensteinormo00shel/page/n5/mode/2up}} The comic also featured a [[Lambda-class T-4a shuttle|''Lambda''-class T4-a shuttle]] used by Tarkin called ''[[PC-265]]'', which shared Cushing's initials and referenced his birthdate of May 26.{{Twitter|cavanscott|status/1181990005662781441|[[Cavan Scott]]|quote=Boom! Adam got the Easter egg!|archivefile=File:TarkinEasterEgg.png}} Scott once again took inspiration from Cushing's horror films in writing the comic story "[[The Haunting of Grand Moff Tarkin]]," which was part of the anthology ''[[Tales from the Death Star]]'' published by [[Dark Horse Comics]] on [[October 17]], [[2023]].{{PenguinComics|url=book/?isbn=9781506738291|text=Star Wars: Tales from the Death Star}} ===Influence on other actors=== {{Quote|I was strongly affected by Peter Cushing, who also portrayed a Moff many, many years ago.|Giancarlo Esposito, on his preparation for the role of Moff Gideon|{{Twitter|starwars|status/1341837863655116800|[[Star Wars]]|quote=Actor @quiethandfilms reflects on becoming the notorious Moff Gideon on #TheMandalorian now streaming on @DisneyPlus.|archivedate=20201223201128}}}} While preparing for the role of [[Moff]] [[Gideon]] in the television series ''[[Star Wars: The Mandalorian]]'', [[Giancarlo Esposito]] took influence from Cushing's performance as a fellow Moff. Gideon first appeared in the [[Chapter 7: The Reckoning|seventh episode]] of the series' [[The Mandalorian Season One|first season]], which debuted on [[Disney+]] on [[December 18]], 2019.{{InsiderCite|201|Star Wars: The Mandalorian Season One Companion|''Star Wars: The Mandalorian'' Season One Companion}} Likewise, during filming of the [[sequel trilogy]] film [[Star Wars: Episode IX The Rise of Skywalker|''Star Wars'': Episode IX ''The Rise of Skywalker'']],{{Twitter|RichardEGrant|status/1202656627511316480|[[Richard E. Grant]]|quote=Took inspiration from the late, great Peter Cushing in @starwars A NEW HOPE to play Allegiant General Pryde in #RiseofSkywalker|archivefile=File:Richard_Grant_Cushing.png}} which premiered on [[December 20]] that year,{{InsiderCite|196|The Jedi Redeemed: Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker}} actor [[Richard E. Grant]] took inspiration of Cushing's portrayal as Tarkin to play [[Allegiant General]] [[Enric Pryde]]. ==Works== ===Filmography=== {| {{Prettytable}} ! Year !! Title !! Contribution(s) !!
